rationale.R1.1: Section advances a specific contribution claim ('first fine-grained, skill-resolved profile') and a gap statement (existing benchmarks conflate competences); verifying the coherence of this thesis is central to any review of the section.
rationale.R1.2: The protocol chain (curation → fixed-T run → global error → mean log-error metric → bootstrap CI) must be internally consistent; logical gaps here would undermine the whole section regardless of review flavor.
rationale.R1.3: Table 2 figures (E = -1.21 for Radau at 1e-9, E = +0.34 for DOPRI5 at 1e-3, normalized wall-clock) are presented as load-bearing evidence for solver rankings; checking that the prose claims map cleanly to what the table can actually support is a core review responsibility.
rationale.R1.4: The closing sentence stakes an absolute novelty claim ('first fine-grained… profile… we recommend NUMSKILL as a standardized replacement'); this is a prominent, falsifiable assertion that any review must probe against the cited prior work (DETEST, Hairer–Wanner testbanks).
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is a plausible primary intent of a bare 'review this section' delegation, covering sentence clarity, precision of technical terms (e.g., 'very tight tolerance'), and paragraph cohesion.
rationale.R1.7: DETEST and the Hairer–Wanner testbanks are named but not formally cited; attribution accuracy is peripherally relevant, especially given the novelty claim depends on characterizing exactly what those prior works do and do not provide.

parsed.clarification_question: Should the review focus primarily on technical correctness and claim validity (logical consistency of the protocol, evidence-to-claim alignment in Table 2, defensibility of the 'first fine-grained profile' novelty claim), or primarily on prose quality and writing style?
